findings.description: reachable() enqueues every neighbor unconditionally without a visited-check, so on the explicitly-supported cyclic graph it loops forever and grows the queue unboundedly.
Even on acyclic input, re-enqueueing already-seen neighbors makes BFS exponential in fan-in/shared-subpaths instead of linear.
reachable() never adds `start` to `seen`, so a node reachable from itself via a cycle (or a self-loop) is incorrectly omitted from the result set.
count_paths() has no cycle guard; a cyclic graph (the 'DAG assumed' precondition is unchecked) causes unbounded recursion and a stack overflow.
count_paths() recomputes shared subpaths with no memoization, yielding exponential blow-up on diamond/DAG structures.
Deep DAGs hit Python's default recursion limit because path counting is recursive rather than iterative.
Indexing the defaultdict (self._edges[node]) for a leaf node inserts an empty-list entry, mutating internal state during a read-only traversal.
count_paths() also indexes the defaultdict for src, inserting spurious empty-list keys as a side effect of a query.
reachable() does not validate that `start` exists in the graph, silently returning an empty set for unknown nodes instead of signaling the error.
Iterable is imported from typing but never used (dead import).
